location: National Building Museum, Washington, D.C., United States
program: Temporary installation / walk-in maze / exhibition preview
status: Temporary exhibition; publicly reported as open July 4 through September 1, 2014
one_sentence_summary: BIG Maze reverses the conventional maze experience by making the path clearer toward the center through gradually lowered walls and expanded visibility.

key_facts.label: Project type
key_facts.value: Temporary walk-in maze installation in the National Building Museum's Great Hall

key_facts.label: Opening and exhibition dates
key_facts.value: Architectural Digest reports opening on July 4, 2014 and display through September 1, 2014.

key_facts.label: Approximate size
key_facts.value: Wired reports a 60 x 60-foot wooden labyrinth; Architectural Digest reports exterior walls reaching 18 feet high.

design_strategies.title: Invert the maze experience
design_strategies.description: Instead of making the route more confusing toward the center, the project increases visual understanding as visitors move inward.

design_strategies.title: Use wall height as an experiential gradient
design_strategies.description: Tall outer walls create enclosure and uncertainty, while lower inner walls open sightlines and reduce anxiety near the center.

design_strategies.title: Turn the center into a point of comprehension
design_strategies.description: The center acts as a vantage point where visitors can see the path they traveled and understand the maze as a whole.

design_strategies.title: Use the museum section as part of the project
design_strategies.description: The Great Hall's upper galleries allow visitors to survey the maze from above, adding an observer view to the bodily experience inside the maze.

spatial_ideas.text: The visitor sequence shifts from narrow, high-walled enclosure to open, low-walled visibility.
The project creates two modes of perception: immersive wandering at floor level and overview from the museum's upper floors.

materials_structure.text: The visible material is neutral-tone maple plywood; detailed structural connections and assembly method are not confirmed by the accessible sources.

site_context.text: The installation relies on the National Building Museum's Great Hall for scale, public access, and elevated viewing positions.

image_links.intended_use: Cover candidate and aerial view reference
image_links.copyright_note: Photo courtesy of the National Building Museum; use as reference link only; rights not cleared.
Use as reference link only; rights not cleared.
Official BIG project page; image rights not cleared.

sources.title: Get Lost in Architect Bjarke Ingels's Soaring Maze
sources.notes: Provides dates, location, material, wall height, Great Hall context, and image credit.

sources.title: A Clever Maze That Actually Gets Less Confusing as You Reach the Center
sources.notes: Provides the 60 x 60-foot scale, inverted-cone wall concept, and visitor-experience framing.

sources.title: Bjarke Ingels Group
sources.notes: Reference-only confirmation that The BIG Maze appears in BIG's 2014 exhibition/project list.

uncertain_or_conflicting_info.description: Architectural Digest text reports '61 square feet', while Wired reports '60 x 60-foot'. The AD figure appears inconsistent with the described 18-foot-high maze and should be checked against official archival material.
The historical National Building Museum project page linked from 2014 media coverage was not reliably accessible during validation.
Accessible sources identify maple plywood but do not confirm detailed structure, joints, fabrication method, budget, or contractor.
